data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Henkel Adds Shine With Olaplex
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Olaplex (OLPX) to be acquired by Henkel (HENKY) for $1.4B; stock surges 49% premarket. (00:14) JetBlue (JBLU) jumps on report it's exploring potential sale. (01:14) LA jury finds Meta, Google liable for inducing social media addiction in young woman. (02:05)

This is an abridged transcript.

Olaplex Holdings (OLPX) is up 49% in premarket action.

Olaplex agreed to be acquired by German consumer giant Henkel AG & Co. KGaA (HENKY) (HELKF) (HENOY).

The all-cash transaction is valued at $1.4B.

The $2.06 per share cash offer represents a massive 55% premium over Olaplex’s March 25 closing price, and a premium of approximately 45% over the volume weighted average price for the last 30 trading days.

Upon completion of the deal, expected in the second half of 2026, Olaplex will be delisted from the Nasdaq but will continue to operate as a standalone brand within Henkel’s professional hair care portfolio.

Notably, Advent International, which holds a majority of the voting power in Olaplex, has already provided written consent for the transaction, so no further action is needed from other stockholders. Advent will fully exit its investment upon closing.

JetBlue (JBLU) closed the day Wednesday up 13% on a report that it's evaluating plans to sell itself to a rival airline.

According to a Semafor report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter, JetBlue (JBLU) has tapped advisers to see how a deal with United Airlines (UAL), Alaska Airlines (ALK) or Southwest Airlines (LUV) might be perceived in Washington.

According to the report, this is preliminary, and the airline may decide to keep the status quo and not try to sell itself. It wasn't known whether JetBlue (JBLU) had held any talks or received indications of interest.

JetBlue declined to comment on internal talks to Semafor.

United (UAL) and Southwest (LUV) also declined to comment. Alaska Air (ALK) didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.

Shares of rival airlines rose after the Semafor report.

A jury in Los Angeles determined Wednesday that Meta Platforms (META) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) were responsible for inducing addictive behavior in a young woman.

She began using social media platforms as a child. The now 20-year-old woman alleged that she became addicted to apps like Instagram and YouTube due to the appearance of the platforms.

She said household social media platforms caused her to develop anxiety, body dysmorphia, and suicidal thoughts. TikTok (BDNCE) and Snap (SNAP) were also part of the suit, but they settled before the case went to court.

The jury ordered compensatory damages of $3M to be awarded to the plaintiff, of which Meta is expected to pay 70% and Google the remaining. Additional punitive damages are also being considered.

A Meta spokesperson said, "We respectfully disagree with the verdict and are evaluating our legal options."

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Trump team reportedly exploring implications of oil reaching $200 per barrel

Iran said to draft bill to impose fees for transit through Strait of Hormuz

Super Micro faces lawsuit alleging securities fraud tied to China sales

Catalyst watch:

  • Shareholders with Heritage Commerce (HTBK) and CVB Financial (CVBF) vote on their all-stock deal valued at about $811M.

  • Shareholders with ON24 (ONTF) will vote on the buyout deal from Cvent.

  • Marex Group (MRX) will hold its Investor Day event.

  • Genius Sports Limited (GENI) will host a strategic NewFront event as part of the official IAB NewFronts week. The NewFront will also feature conversations with leaders from Publicis Sports, NBCUniversal (CMCSA), the NFL and WNBA, Lowe's (LOW), and others, exploring how live sports is evolving into a real-time, data-powered, and addressable media environment.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 4% at $93. Bitcoin is down 2% at $69,000. Gold is down 2% at $4,429.

The FTSE 100 is down 1% and the DAX is down 1.5%. The market in India closed for a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: MillerKnoll (MLKN) -19% – Shares plunged after the company reported Q3 results that missed expectations, with adjusted EPS of $0.43 and revenue of $926.6M, both below consensus.

Economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Starbucks: Full Price For A Half-Brewed Turnaround
data.description:
At the current stock price of $92, Starbucks (SBUX) is trading at a GAAP P/E (TTM) multiple of 76.6x and a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 40.2x. We think these multiples assume a full recovery of margins that, in our view, the market is taking for granted prematurely. Q1 FY2026 showed significant improvements in the top line, with total revenue up 6% and comparable sales up 4% YoY, but operating margin kept compressing with a 290 bps decline. Also, non-GAAP EPS declined 19%, and the dividend seems unsustainable at a payout ratio of 123.6%. A straightforward multiples valuation and a DCF model suggest an intrinsic value of $73 for the stock, which implies a 21% downside. In our view, the market is paying a full turnaround price for a turnaround that has barely begun. Our rating is a sell.

Top-Line Recovery While Margins Still Lag

Starbucks Corporation operates over 41,000 coffeehouses across more than 80 countries, generating $37.7 billion in annual revenue and employing around 381,000 people. The company operates through three segments: North America (74% of revenue), International (21%), and Channel Development (5%, including the Global Coffee Alliance with Nestlé and the RTD collaboration with PepsiCo). While it is by far the world's most recognized coffee brand, brand awareness and investment returns are two very different things, and that is precisely where the story gets complicated.

After years of consistent growth, at a 7.8% CAGR between 2019 and 2023, SBUX's narrative shifted in FY2024. That year, comparable sales declined 2%, with Q4 transactions plummeting by 10% in North America. In August, the board replaced the CEO with Brian Niccol, a talented executive who was the architect of Chipotle's (CMG) turnaround. In FY2025, the deterioration continued as operating margin fell to 7.9% from 15% the previous year, 627 stores were closed in Q4, 1,100 corporate positions were eliminated, and share buybacks were suspended.

Q1 2026 showed a better top-line performance, with revenue coming in at $9.9 billion, which was 6% higher than the previous year, and the first increase in total transactions (4%) in the US in eight quarters. However, that improvement did not translate to profitability, as non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 was 19% lower than the previous year and was also below analysts' expectations of $0.59. In terms of margins, the operating margin fell another 290 bps to 9%, while the net income margin of 3% was well below the 8.3% reported in Q1 2025. In other words, Starbucks is selling more coffee but making less money on each cup. In our view, the market is celebrating the first part of the equation but ignoring the second.

The 2026 Investor Day demonstrated that Niccol has ambitious plans for the company. He laid out a plan towards FY2028 EPS of $3.35-$4.00, revenue growth of 5%+, and more than 2,000 new stores. Also, as part of its turnaround program, in November the company had announced the joint venture with Boyu Capital, by which SBUX retains a 40% minority interest and sells the remaining 60% to Boyu for $4 billion. Niccol declared, 'Starbucks is back,' but to reach the EPS target of $3.35-$4.00 from the current $2.13 (FY 2025), the company needs to expand margins by roughly 400 bps in three years while simultaneously spending an additional $500 million in labor hours, negotiating with unions that are demanding significant wage increases, and facing headwinds from coffee prices at historically high levels.

Losing Market Share in a Crowded Space

The global coffee market is massive, with an estimated size of $249 billion in 2025 and a CAGR of 5.4% through 2033, according to Grand View Research. America is drinking more coffee, but competition is intensifying, and according to Technomic data, SBUX is losing market share, from 52% in 2023 to 48% last year. Dutch Bros, 7 Brew, and Scooter's Coffee are rapidly growing in the drive-through segment, and the Chinese tea brand Mixue is entering the US market with low prices and a franchise model that can lead to a rapid expansion in the years to come. As an analyst quoted by AP said, " People haven't fallen out of love with Starbucks, but they're now polyamorous in their coffee choices." In China, competition is also stiff, with Luckin Coffee being the leader with its 100% digital orders and pick-up stores and super low-price chain Cotti Coffee now having 16,000 stores, compared to approximately 8,000 Starbucks locations. This likely explains the company's decision to reduce its exposure by selling 60% of its Chinese business and shifting to a joint venture structure.

We do not deny Starbucks' moat exists. The firm owns an iconic brand, a best-in-class rewards program with over 35 million 90-day active members, and a scale of more than 40,000 stores that is hard to replicate. In addition, at the 2026 Investor Day, Niccol introduced some real operational innovations, like the peak throughput below 4 minutes, 25,000 new seats for 2026, a reinvented three-tier reward program, and the new Mastrena 3 espresso machine. But the moat is eroding as market share declines and consumers have more alternatives than ever. Also, Starbucks' most valuable competitive advantages, such as the brand, premium locations, and digital ecosystem, are already priced into the rich multiple the stock is currently trading at. In essence, the question is not whether SBUX is a good business, as it clearly is, but whether it is a good investment at current price levels, which we believe it is not.

Starbucks is trading at a forward P/E ratio of 40.2x with an EBIT margin of 9.35% and low single-digit growth, a premium that is hard to justify. McDonald's (MCD), due to its business model, has margins that are 4.9x higher and trades at 23x. Luckin Coffee (LKNCY) grows at 27% YoY with comparable margins but trades at a 16.2x multiple. Dutch Bros (BROS) is more expensive on a P/E basis, but it is growing at 25%. It seems clear that SBUX offers the worst balance of growth, profitability, and valuation in the entire group.

Unions, consumer sentiment, and gasoline prices

Beyond competition, unionization represents another material weakness. Starbucks Workers United union, which has organized more than 700 stores, is demanding a $20/hour minimum wage plus 5% annual raises. Persistent labor disputes put pressure on margins and increase the firm's reputational risk.

Weak consumer sentiment is another factor that the market might be underestimating. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index declined to a 55.5 reading in March, a historically low print for the series. Further, rising gasoline prices due to the military conflict with Iran are weighing on household financial expectations. This environment is highly negative for Starbucks, as $6 coffee is exactly the type of discretionary expense that is cut when disposable income compresses. The EY-Parthenon Consumer Sentiment Survey of March 2026 confirms that consumers are prioritizing essentials and pausing or deferring discretionary categories, while budget retailers are gaining traffic as customers trade down from premium shops.

Rising gasoline prices amid global crude supply shock due to the military conflict are weighing on inflation expectations. This month, one-year inflation expectations stalled at 3.4%, ending a six-month streak of declines. However, nearly half of the interviews were conducted before the start of the military conflict. If the trend in gasoline prices continues, it could have a cascading effect, including lower disposable income, increased pressure on input costs (food and coffee), and intensified union demand for wage hikes to offset the loss of purchasing power. This results in a difficult choice for SBUX: raising prices when customers are trading down to cheaper alternatives or accepting lower margins. It is a vicious cycle for a company that sells premium in an environment where premium becomes luxury.

No Margin of Safety At 40x Earnings

To value the stock, we can start with a simple exercise. Based on the midpoint FY2026 EPS guidance of $2.28, the current $92 stock price implies a forward P/E ratio of 40.4x, significantly above SBUX's 5-year average of 29x. For the stock to be fairly priced under these historical terms, it should be trading at $66. Bulls will argue that the FY2028 guidance sets a midpoint EPS of $3.68, but this is a target, and it assumes everything goes right. Even if we discount the $3.68 EPS two years back to present value at a 9% cost of capital proxy, we get $3.10 today. At current prices, this means a forward P/E ratio of 29.7x. In essence, at a stock price of $92, the market is paying full price for the management's optimistic turnaround scenario. There is no margin of safety.

A simple DCF with conservative yet reasonable assumptions - 9% cost of capital, a P/E exit multiple of 24x, $2.5 billion in annual capex, and operating margins gradually normalizing to 14% by FY2030 - yields an intrinsic value of approximately $73 per share. This implies a 21% downside from current levels. Even assuming the turnaround is fully successful and margins return to 15%, the fair value would be $95, which is close to where the stock trades today. In our view, this confirms that the market has already priced in an optimistic scenario.

Risks and catalysts

Our bearish thesis could be wrong for several reasons. First, if Niccol executes the margins turnaround sooner than anticipated, for example, if non-GAAP operating margins exceed 14% by FY2027, EPS would be close to $3, making the current multiple more justifiable. Second, if the Chinese joint venture with Boyu leads to a re-rating based on a more asset-light business model. Third, if the $2 billion savings plan more than offsets additional labor investments. Fourth, if the new three-tier rewards program successfully drives a boost in traffic and spending. Finally, if the US consumer proves more resilient than anticipated despite deteriorating sentiment. While these risks are real and should be monitored, we believe that at current valuations, the asymmetric risks favor the downside.

At the same time, there are some catalysts that could push the stock price to lower levels, such as the Q2 FY2026 earnings release showing continued pressure on margins, analysts downgrades, or a further deterioration of the US macroeconomic environment and consumer sentiment.

The bottom line

Investing is not just about business quality; it is also about the price that investors pay for that quality. At $92 per share, investors are paying a TTM GAAP P/E multiple of 76.6x and a forward non-GAAP multiple of 40.2x, for a company with an EBIT margin of 9.35%, a dividend payout ratio of 123.6%, declining market share, a consumer under growing pressure, and a turnaround plan that has not yet shown improvements in profitability. Based on our DCF, the fair value of the stock is around $73, implying a 21% downside. Even the analysts' consensus, which tends to be optimistic, points to $100, offering no margin of safety at current prices.

Consequently, our rating is a Sell. For investors who want exposure to this turnaround story, we recommend waiting for the stock price to retrace to a level where the risk/reward ratio becomes more balanced. The turnaround may be real, but at the current price, the market is paying as if it were already completed.

data.title: MercadoLibre: Buy Latin America's Leading E-Commerce And Fintech Compounder
data.description:
MercadoLibre (MELI) is often described as the "Amazon of Latin America," and that isn't completely wrong. MELI holds a dominant and expanding market share in e-commerce, even outcompeting Amazon and cheap Chinese competitors. The other portion of MELI is Mercado Pago, which is quickly disrupting traditional banks.

Their Q4 earnings proved they are receiving great ROI, accelerating revenue growth to 45% and driven by all segments. The stock tumbled because of the EPS miss headline, driven by margin compression from growth investments, bringing MELI down roughly 35% from its all-time highs. However, this is a deliberate strategy, echoing Amazon's iconic early philosophy of prioritizing scale and market share over short-term profitability. For example, MELI recently lowered free shipping barriers to customers, which caused the multi-year record growth:

I rank MELI as a strong buy for long-term investors, as it's an incredibly fast-growing company trading at an unwarranted discount.

Business Overview and Moat

The economics of logistics comes from scale. MercadoLibre has the first-mover advantage, and management is pressing the gas pedal. Recently MELI announced it is investing $3.4b in Argentina for 2026, preparing for immense growth while sacrificing short-term margins.

Mercado Envios is its proprietary logistics network. A decade ago, e-commerce in Latin America consisted of notoriously slow and unreliable national postal services (such as Correios in Brazil). Instead of relying on third-party shippers like Amazon often does in emerging markets, MELI spent billions aggressively building an in-house fulfillment network from scratch. This includes dedicated distribution centers, truck fleets, and even cargo planes. Today, Mercado Envios handles almost all items sold on the platform, in very competitive delivery times. This capital-intensive infrastructure is very difficult for new entrants or foreign competitors like Shopee to replicate overnight. MELI uses their infrastructure for both 3P and 1P products.

Being the leading e-commerce platform in many countries means they have the most merchants, fast deliveries, and a subscription (MELI+) to tie it into the best value for consumers. MELI GMV has grown 26% on average in the past decade, faster than the general LATAM total addressable market. Brazil accounts for half of revenue, Argentina and Mexico 22% each, and other South American countries for the rest.

Then MELI can funnel demand into Mercado Pago. For example, a merchant needs to make a Mercado Pago account to sell on MercadoLibre, or a consumer to use credit on MercadoLibre. As of Q4, MELI has around 79m active Mercado Pago MAUs. Those MAUs have lower acquisition costs, as a lot of them flow from the 121m MercadoLibre consumers. They are locked in with the MELI+ subscription, which includes free shipping, cashback, and even streaming services like Netflix.

Other than AWS, Amazon's profit driver is advertising. While MELI doesn't have a cloud business, advertising is a major bottom line driver to layer over its e-commerce platform. For 2025, it generated $1.54b of revenue while growing 50%+. Even with aggressive infrastructure reinvestment in the core businesses, advertising will add meaningfully to the bottom line.

Why is MELI seeing so much growth? Necessity. Latin America was lacking effective delivery logistics and financial services. Mercado Pago is not only stealing market share from legacy banks but also expanding the market by giving millions of people their first digital wallet or credit card. This is a substantial part of MELI, making up nearly 45% of revenue and 79m monthly users. Because of its massive e-commerce ecosystem, MELI has rich data to build proprietary credit risk models, making defaults highly manageable. Last quarter MELI had a 23.3% NIMAL (Net Interest Margin After Losses), earning a solid bottom line. Beyond credit issuance, the true institutional value of Mercado Pago lies in its rapidly evolving funding structure. Normally, fintechs have to borrow expensive capital to fund consumer credit or loans. However, Mercado Pago has successfully transitioned into a massive deposit-gathering machine. By offering attractive market-rate yields on parked cash (like tying accounts to the CDI rate in Brazil), users are incentivized to use Mercado Pago as their primary bank account. This gives MELI a massive, incredibly cheap base of customer deposits to fund its lucrative merchant and consumer credit portfolios. This self-funding loop drastically lowers their cost of capital and acts as a massive tailwind for future net interest margin expansion:

Financials

MELI has grown revenue above 30% for 28 consecutive quarters. Usually companies quickly decelerate growth as they scale. Despite a ~$29b revenue run rate, MELI's top line has accelerated over the past few quarters to 45%; that would double revenue every 2 years.

While aggressive investments into distribution centers or their credit portfolio might temporarily lower margins, long-term operating leverage and higher margins will drive faster bottom-line growth for businesses.

Valuation

MELI currently trades around a 40x P/E. While this might appear high at first, it is highly attractive after factoring in growth. For 2027 estimates, MELI only trades at a 21x P/E. MELI currently makes $39.39 in trailing EPS. I will assume a 30% EPS CAGR over the next five years. This is a conservative deceleration from their recent 40%+ top-line growth, factoring in the natural law of large numbers, but acknowledging the massive operating leverage gained as high-margin segments (Ads and Credit) increasingly outpace the traditional retail business. By 2030, the EPS will be approximately $146. If growth decelerates faster or risks materialize, there is plenty of margin of safety to fall back on.

Applying a 30x terminal P/E multiple, a common premium for a dominant and fast-growing company, yields a 2030 stock price of ~$4,400. Or a 22% five year CAGR from today's price of $1630. My hurdle rate for stocks is 15%+, better than the usual 10% from the S&P 500. That would mean MELI's fair value is currently $2200 with 35% imminent upside.

Risks

LATAM is less stable than the US and usually trades at a discount. Rapid FX headwinds can reduce results even with great operations. Political uncertainty, higher interest rates, high inflation, and other things come with these developing countries. However, even during hyperinflation times like in Argentina, people flock to high-yield Mercado Pago accounts to protect their money. Additionally, competition from Amazon, Shopee, or legacy banks is always applying pressure. This is MELI's game to lose.

Conclusion

MercadoLibre is scaling a dominant ecosystem that acts as the primary commerce and financial tollbooth for Latin America. The market is mispricing MELI because of aggressive reinvestments in its proprietary logistics and fintech infrastructure. However, it is a deliberate decision to accelerate growth and strengthen its moat. I rank MELI as a Strong Buy with a 1-year price target of at least $2200. Its current 40x P/E is a huge historical discount; while sounding high at first, it quickly becomes a bargain with revenue growing 40%+. For long-term investors, this is an opportunity to buy MELI at an implied 21x 2027 P/E.

data.title: Columbia Cornerstone Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Cornerstone Equity Fund Institutional Class shares returned 3.27% for the three months ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, returned 2.66% over the same period.
  • In sector terms, positioning within health care, financials, consumer discretionary and communication services led contributions to relative performance, while positioning within information technology and industrials weighed most heavily on performance.

Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.66% during the fourth quarter, as measured by the S&P 500 Index. The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble." Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year, as value stocks outpaced their growth counterparts.

Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Microsoft -0.55

ServiceNow (NOW) -0.22
AT&T (T) -0.19

Quarterly portfolio recap of performance relative to the benchmark

In sector terms, positioning within health care, financials, consumer discretionary and communication services led contributions to relative performance, while positioning within information technology and industrials weighed most heavily on performance.

Within health care, positive contributions were led by Exact Sciences, as the provider of a home screening test for colon cancer was acquired by Abbott Laboratories (ABT) at a significant premium to its trading price. Eli Lilly was another standout performer, posting strong earnings driven by its dominant position in obesity treatments, while providing favorable updates on an oral weight loss drug in development.

Within financials, overweight exposure to large banks, including Citigroup (C), Goldman Sachs (GS) and Bank of America (BAC), proved beneficial. Sentiment around these stocks benefited from strong earnings reports driven by increased capital markets activity and strong trading results. In addition, an improving regulatory outlook should support continued capital returns to shareholders.

Within consumer discretionary, automaker General Motors posted strong earnings and raised its full-year earnings guidance above consensus estimates, leading the stock higher. In addition, better-than-expected data with respect to tariff costs and sales of larger, more profitable vehicles such as trucks and SUVs supported the stock.

Alphabet highlighted contributions within communication services. The provider of the Google search engine had been viewed as a potential “AI loser,” but has flipped that narrative based on the impressive performance of its Gemini generative-AI assistant and share gains for its large-language model used to generate predictive text. In addition, Alphabet reported strong advertising revenue from its Google search and YouTube streaming segments.

On the downside, within information technology, shares of ServiceNow declined during the quarter on news that the workplace-productivity-platform provider planned to acquire cybersecurity company Armis. The market appeared to react to the short-term impact of the deal on earnings and the potential challenges around integration. We believe these concerns are overblown and view the acquisition as strategically sound with respect to ServiceNow's existing capabilities and growth opportunities. Shares of cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks (PANW) also moved lower on news of an agreement to acquire Chronosphere, a provider of "observability" software used to diagnose IT system issues. While the deal comes with some execution risk and near-term pressure on free cash flow, we believe the acquisition fits well with Palo Alto's strategy and opens a new avenue of growth. Database software and enterprise application provider Oracle was another laggard. The company posted results in keeping with expectations and reported a strong order backlog. However, investors have become concerned about the company's rising capital expenditures and increasing debt financing to support the company's significant backlog of cloud infrastructure contracts, as well as customer concentration risk, given its massive long-term deal to support OpenAI's data centers. We view the growth trajectory for Oracle as attractive and expect that improving margins will support higher free cash flow than anticipated and help ease concerns around financing.

Within industrials, power management company Eaton (ETN) underperformed after providing guidance that was disappointing relative to high expectations. Eaton remains well positioned to benefit from the buildout of AI data centers, which require intense power use and advanced technology to dissipate heat. We expect the company to experience accelerated growth as it integrates recent acquisitions and recent capacity additions begin to contribute to revenue.

While selection was generally positive within communication services, AT&T was among the leading individual detractors, as initially sluggish sales for the latest iPhone model and disappointing revenue from postpaid mobile phone plans weighed on the stock. Social media company Meta Platforms was another laggard, with shares pulling back on the announcement of plans to significantly increase spending on efforts to develop artificial general intelligence and integrate it across its platforms.

Outlook

We remain constructive on the outlook for equities, based on improving business confidence, a renewed Fed easing cycle and economic tailwinds to capital spending from the recently passed budget bill. Signs of a recovery in lending and capital markets activity further support the outlook. Risks around labor market softening and the impact of tariffs remain, but appear manageable in the context of broader economic resilience, especially if inflation remains contained.

More broadly, while seeking to construct a portfolio that can be resilient to a range of economic outcomes, we continue to focus on stock selection as the driver of returns. In so doing, we seek a combination of certain characteristics that we believe has the potential to outperform throughout a market cycle. We emphasize companies with strong free-cash-flow generation, improving revenue and earnings trends, high or rising returns on invested capital and sound or improving balance sheets.

data.title: Invesco Rochester Limited Term New York Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Rochester® Limited Term New York Municipal Fund Class A (LTNYX) shares at net asset value (NAV) outperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal New York Investment Grade 4-7 Years Bond Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the fourth quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 1.42%, 1.11% and 1.05%, with annual returns of 4.25%, 2.46% and 7.89%, respectively. 1 Despite a lengthy federal government shutdown, long-duration municipal bonds performed well during the quarter, bolstering market strength. 1
  • New municipal issuance reached $143 billion for the quarter and a record $584 billion for the year, surpassing last year's record $509 billion. Shifting interest rate policies, higher costs and political uncertainty likely encouraged more issuers to come to market. 1
  • Net flows for municipal mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) were strong, totaling approximately $17.5 billion for the quarter and $52.4 billion for the year. 2
  • The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the federal funds rate twice, by 0.25% in October and 0.25% in December. The Fed reiterated its commitment to balancing maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. 3
  • We believe state and local municipal budgets remain healthy. While credit rating upgrades have moderated, upgrades outpaced downgrades in 2025, demonstrating to us strong fundamentals. 4
  • Our experienced credit research team continues to seek out market dislocations, uncovering opportunities to add value for shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see attractive opportunities in municipals. With prospects for more Fed interest rate cuts, steady issuance and ongoing demand for tax-exempt income, we believe high absolute yields and solid fundamentals make municipals a compelling choice.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for duration and are looking to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate and according to prospectus guidelines. We maintain an overweight in the shorter maturity segment of the yield curve given the fund's short duration mandate.

Sector allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds, though we note that pension risks historically associated with general obligation bonds have generally been mitigated over the past several years. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the dedicated tax, higher education and airport sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market, as credit rating upgrades have continued to outpace downgrades. While various sectors have come under pressure due to reduced federal support, inflation-driven cost pressures and negative political headlines, we continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to identify strong credits with attractive yields.

Performance highlights

The fourth quarter began on a strong note despite record levels of new issuance and the longest federal government shutdown in history. Economic signals were mixed, with solid Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth alongside persistent inflation and a weakening employment outlook. The Fed cut the federal funds target rate by 0.25% in both October and December, for a total of three rate cuts in 2025. Municipals rallied in October and delivered steady, positive returns through the end of the quarter. Yields on the short end of the municipal yield curve rose, with two- and five-year bond yields rising by 0.17% and 0.08%, respectively. 5 Overall, higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed during the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's overweight in bonds with durations between seven and 15 years contributed to relative performance. Security selection among bonds rated AA and higher, where the fund is underweight, added to relative performance, as did security selection in the dedicated tax sector.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection among zero-coupon bonds, specifically a position in zero-coupon tobacco bonds, detracted from relative performance.

data.title: John Hancock Regional Bank Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index—the fund's secondary benchmark—returned 3.15% in the fourth quarter and finished 2026 with a gain of 10.63%.
  • The fund returned 5.05% and 11.39% in the quarter and the year, respectively, outpacing the benchmark in both time periods.
  • Individual stock selection was the primary driver of the fund's positive three-month results.

Market review and outlook

Regional bank stocks performed well in the quarter, closing out a positive year. The sector continued to benefit from the combination of steady economic conditions, healthy earnings, and expectations that AI adoption will help reduce banks' costs over the longer term. In addition, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) enacted two quarter-point interest rate cuts, bringing the total for the year to three. The Fed also announced the end of its multi-year quantitative tightening program, which had involved the gradual reduction of the securities on its balance sheet. Together, these factors helped regional banks post a solid gain and outperform the broader U.S. equity market in the final three months of 2025.

Healthy fundamentals also provided a catalyst for the sector's performance. Banks exhibited solid profitability, supported by strong capital and liquidity, and their credit costs remained low. A more benign regulatory backdrop contributed to a recent increase in merger-and-acquisition activity, providing a further boost to the sector. Most banks' net interest margins have risen, and we think they can continue to expand as fixed-rate assets reprice higher. This means that the portion of loan books originated during the low-rate environment of 2020 to 2022 will mature and be replaced with much higher-rate loans over the next few years, creating a tailwind for revenues.

We believe net interest margin expansion, coupled with loan growth, should drive healthy revenue gains in 2026. Wall Street analysts now project double-digit revenue growth for the year ahead, a favorable level given the sector's attractive valuation. At the end of December, regional banks traded at a price-to-earnings ratio of approximately 9.6 times 2026 estimates, and price-to-book value stood at 1.21x. These are significant discounts to both history and the broader U.S. equity market.

Most banks' underwriting continued to be conservative despite persistent macro uncertainty, and their credit costs remained benign. The average small to mid-size bank reported quarterly net charge-offs of only 15 basis points (0.15%), a historically low level. Moreover, regional banks have robust capital levels that should allow them to maintain loan growth and return capital to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

Contributors and detractors

Comerica, Inc. (CMA), which was acquired at a premium by Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), was the leading individual contributor to the fund's fourth-quarter relative performance. U.S. Bancorp (USB), whose management provided an upbeat revenue outlook that was supported by improving loan growth expectations, also contributed to results. KeyCorp (KEY) was another top performer. Its management delivered a positive update at an industry conference and announced a larger-than-expected share buyback. Additionally, KeyCorp downplayed the possibility of acquiring another bank in alignment with an activist investor's call to prioritize internal improvements and strengthen organic returns.

Nicolet Bankshares, Inc. (NIC), whose shares fell after it announced the acquisition of MidWestOne (MOFG) in late October, was the largest detractor. We maintained the position on the view that the deal creates an attractive franchise and will be accretive to 2026 earnings after anticipated cost savings. Bank7 Corp. (BSVN) and Renasant Corp. (RNST) also detracted from results.

Portfolio changes

Our activity was fairly light in the quarter. We eliminated one holding—Prosperity Bancshares, Inc. (PB)—and added no new positions.

data.title: Burlington Stores: The Next Leg Of Upside Is From Earnings Growth
data.description:
Investment action

I reiterated my buy rating for Burlington Stores (BURL) previously, as I believed the SSSG weakness was not structural and that it would recover to mid-single-digits by Q4. What has changed so far is that SSSG rebounded to 4% in Q4 2025 despite a weather hit and a deliberate pullback in tariff-pressured categories, while margins still expanded nicely. The margin expansion story has also gotten better.

Reported sales understate how good demand really was

To start, I think it is important for readers to realize that BURL did much better than the headline numbers in the recent results update. Firstly, a 4% SSSG on top of 6% last year is already strong. But that could have been much better, as management said that BURL could have done more sales in Q4, especially in toys, gifting, housewares, bedding, and seasonal décor. The issue was not that customers were not buying. The issue was that BURL had already cut back its plans in businesses where tariffs had made the margin math less attractive. So this was a choice made to “grow less” to protect margins, not that demand was weak. In hindsight, it would have been great for BURL to capture those demands, as absolute earnings growth is more important than margins (in my opinion). Nonetheless, this tells us that the consumer demand backdrop is strong, and BURL could have printed low teens two-year stacked SSSG.

Another element to consider is the winter storm. Management noted that November and December ran at a mid-single-digit SSSG. January also ran at a mid-single-digit SSSG pace before the winter storm disrupted several hundred stores. The estimate provided by management was that the storm cost ~100 bps of SSSG for the Q4 quarter and several points for January. Then February reverted back on track (I assume this meant back to a mid-single-digit pace).

If we combine that self-imposed pullback to not capture lower-margin demand plus the weather disruption, I think it is safe to say that BURL would’ve printed a new recent high SSSG of potentially high single digits. The quality of this growth also matters. This was not driven by a single pocket of customers. Management specifically noted Q4 trends were broad-based across income cohorts and across ethnic trade areas. Lower-income areas actually did slightly better, but only slightly. This narrative does help to push back against the idea that BURL is only doing well because it benefits from the trade-down motion.

Margin expansion

The margin expansion story also looks much better now. I believe the margin expansion BURL achieved this past quarter was structural, and there should be more room to expand. Part of that came from the back-end initiatives such as better freight planning and lower shortages. But one of the more important initiatives is on the merchandise side, the elevation strategy. In simple terms, that means BURL has been moving the assortment up by offering better and more recognizable brands, higher quality, and more fashion, while still keeping the value proposition intact. This has worked really well, as BURL is seeing higher customer perception scores, stronger SSSG in the higher price buckets, a mid-single-digit increase in average unit retail, and also higher transaction size. Clearly, it is not that lower-income customers don’t want branded goods; they are willing to pay if the price is right, and BURL has surely done a right job pricing them.

Another margin expansion catalyst is localization. For context, this means buying and allocating with more precision, which also means there will be a lower percentage of inventories sitting on the shelf (not sold) and fewer markdowns later on. Management themselves have noted this as a major opportunity and admitted BURL is behind peers. We have to see how well BURL can execute here, but the preparations are mostly done, as BURL has built enough planning and allocation capability to start pushing harder here. To note a few, BURL now has better store planning, stronger localization analytics, and new store assortment planning and is anchoring assortment decisions to demographic ones. If this works, we should see continuous uplift of SSSG, which should flow down nicely to the margin line.

Valuation

As I expected, BURL’s SSSG accelerated back to mid-single-digits, and the stock reacted very positively. Back in November, the stock traded at the -1 standard deviation level (~24x NTM PE) but is now at ~28x NTM PE. The relative discount vs. peers has also closed to 4% (from 9%). From here, the upside is less multiple re-rating and more earnings, which is still attractive. With mid-single-digit SSSG existing in FY2025 and the number of stores open still growing at high-single-digits, we could easily bridge to low-to-mid-teen revenue growth. At that pace of growth, even with margins flat, adj. EPS could easily grow in the north of 10% (probably high teens since there would be some form of margin expansion from all the fixed costs).

Using consensus estimates (high-teens adj. EPS CAGR), BURL is expected to deliver ~$15.9 adj. EPS in FY28, and at 28x, that implies a share price target of $445.2.

Risk

BURL now has more moving parts to control within the business. It has the elevation strategy, the localization initiatives, and also the new Savannah distribution center to ramp up. If localization does not improve allocation as expected, or if the DC startup creates more disruption or cost than planned, the margin expansion may not play out. This could really weigh on the stock sentiment, especially with BURL now trading at 28x NTM PE.

Conclusion

I give BURL a buy rating. This quarter confirmed that BURL SSSG can return to mid-single-digit growth (and beyond). It also showed that underlying demand was stronger than reported and that the margin expansion story is getting more structural. The stock multiple is no longer as cheap as it was a few months ago, so I agree the next leg of upside is likely to come more from earnings than multiple rerating.

data.title: Middle East War Pressures Copper Prices, Could Impact Nickel Production
data.description:
Due to the broadening conflict in the Middle East, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz on Feb. 28 led to increased maritime risks for oil and energy markets. With over 25% of global seaborne oil and about 20% of global LNG trade passing through the strait, this stalled trade route furthers cost-push inflation given supply and logistical uncertainties.

In this article, we highlight the closure's impacts to the copper and nickel markets, with the latter becoming increasingly susceptible to supply-side shocks.

Copper prices tempered by US dollar

The escalation of the US-Iran conflict is expected to have a limited effect on global copper supply. Although Iran is the Middle East's main copper producer, its output - about 360,000 metric tons of mined copper and 340,000 mt of refined copper in 2025 - represents just 1.5% and 1.2% of global production, respectively.

In 2025, Iran exported just over 109,000 mt of refined copper, mainly to the United Arab Emirates and Turkey. Both countries can easily source copper from alternative suppliers, minimizing the risk of disruption.

Global refined copper stocks have increased significantly in major exchange warehouses since January, rising 50% by early March to 1.25 million mt. This inventory buildup provides a buffer against potential supply shocks from the region.

The London Metal Exchange three-month (LME 3M) copper price fell from $13,343.5/mt on Feb. 27 to $12,955/mt on March 3, suggesting the conflict's immediate impact on copper has been downward. The closure of the strait has triggered a rapid increase in energy prices, which is expected to weaken global growth and put pressure on copper demand.

At the same time, higher energy prices have renewed concerns about inflation, potentially delaying any US Federal Reserve interest rate cuts. This has strengthened the US dollar recently, adding further downward pressure on copper prices.

High global refined copper stocks are also weighing on prices, but this could be a short-term phenomenon. With the Chinese market now back from the Lunar New Year holiday, copper-consuming sectors are resuming production and gradually entering their seasonal peak demand period.

As a result, we anticipate stocks at the Shanghai Futures Exchange will begin to decline from current high levels. Most global copper stocks are now sitting in the US market and are unlikely to flow out easily, especially with the possibility of the US imposing tariffs on refined copper imports.

In summary, copper prices are currently under short-term pressure from a stronger dollar, high inventories and demand concerns should global GDP growth weaken. Looking ahead, potential dollar weakness and ongoing tightness in the concentrates market are expected to provide support for copper prices.

Nickel supply chain vulnerable given Hormuz closure

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz highlighted a critical vulnerability within the global nickel value chain: the availability and cost stability of sulfur-based inputs essential for high-pressure acid leach (HPAL) operations. HPAL technology is a vital processing stage in the production of battery-grade nickel sulfate (NiSO₄) used in electric vehicle batteries.

It relies heavily on sulfuric acid to convert laterite ore into mixed hydroxide precipitate (MHP), which is subsequently turned into NiSO₄. Thus, disruptions in the sulfur supply chain can materially impact primary nickel supply and even exacerbate price volatility.

According to S&P Global Energy's preliminary assessment, Indonesia imported over 5.2 million mt of sulfur in 2025, up by 44% year over year, driven by the accelerated expansion of HPAL output. Exports to Indonesia from Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE jumped 29%, led by outflows toward Bahodopi and Weda Bay, both of which house HPAL plants.

Bahodopi, a joint venture between PT Vale Indonesia Tbk (PTNDF) and GEM Hong Kong International Co. Ltd., is expected to commence operations within the year. On the other hand, PT Weda Bay Nickel received a lower production quota of 12 million wet metric tons for 2026, from 42 million wmt in 2025. Eramet SA (ERMAF), one of the venturers alongside Tsingshan Holding Group Co. Ltd. and PT Aneka Tambang Tbk (PAEKY), stated that the company will apply for a quota increase.

Although the recent rally in LME 3M nickel prices within the $17,000/mt to $18,000/mt range - partly attributed to Indonesia's recent quota reduction announcements - has improved market sentiment, it does not fully offset the impact of rising sulfur costs.

HPAL plants with higher all‑in sustaining costs (AISC) in regions such as Australia and New Caledonia are more vulnerable during a surge in sulfur prices. These plants often struggle with design limitations, slow ramp‑up performance and persistently high operating costs.

By contrast, Indonesia's HPAL operations benefit from structural advantages that keep their AISC low. They operate with lower energy and labor costs and tend to achieve higher recoveries through newer-generation HPAL designs.

Should the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz persist, the nickel market will likely begin to increase the price premium associated with battery-grade nickel chemicals. The next phase of nickel market volatility may be driven less by ore supply constraints and more by the resilience of the sulfur-to-acid supply chain that underpins HPAL operations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corporation Isn't Worth A Hoot
data.description:
High yield opportunities can be difficult to resist. Even though I myself don't care much about payouts to investors, even I acknowledge that if the yield is high enough, it can be difficult to say no to. Unfortunately, there are many opportunities that exist out there in the market that offer large payouts but that are, on the whole, subpar investments. One great example of this has historically been Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). Today, the company offers a yield of 13.6%. Considering that the S&P 500 usually averages between 10% and 11% annually, that looks promising at face value.

Even so, back in December of last year, I ended up rating this enterprise a ‘sell.’ I was discouraged by a couple of things. For starters, the vast majority of the debt that the company owned, which comprised about 83% of its portfolio at the time, was floating rate. That meant that, as interest rates dropped, it should see a decline in profitability. But on top of this, the enterprise already had a track record of underperforming the market. And this wasn't just over a short window of time. It was actually over the course of many years. With interest rates slated to drop, I argued that further downward pressure could be put onto its profitability. And while we have not actually seen that come to pass, my overall stance on the matter remains unchanged. Add on top of this a little bit of drama that recently broke out regarding its family of businesses, and I think that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate is the right move here.

Digging into Blue Owl Capital

At its core, Blue Owl Capital is a conceptually interesting company. The company exists for the primary purpose of originating and making loans to middle-market companies in the US. These are primarily smaller, privately held businesses, that are trying to access capital for cheaper than what other alternatives might be. At the end of 2025, the company had investments in 234 different businesses. These were spread across 30 different industries, with the largest being the Internet software and services space at 11.1% of total exposure. Other major categories include health care providers and services, asset based lending and fund financing firms, insurance businesses, health care technology, manufacturing, and food and beverage enterprises. It is worth noting that the Internet software and services portion of the company's emphasis could be very appealing if forecasts regarding AI turn out to be accurate. In 2023, global cloud spending amounted to $496 billion. By 2030 it is expected to explode to $2 trillion. However, as I detailed in another article recently, there does appear to be an AI bubble that, if I turn out to be correct, could lead to increased risk for the business.

The middle market space is actually quite large. Even if we just focus on the US, there are an estimated 200,000 such companies that employ around 48 million people. In fact, these businesses collectively account for around one-third of private sector GDP. Management specifically targets those businesses that generate EBITDA of between $25 million and $500 million, as well as those that generate revenue of between $125 million and $5 billion. This is primarily what you would consider to be the upper middle market space. This excludes firms that, by definition, can be as small as $10 million per year worth of EBITDA. But by focusing on these more sizable enterprises, the overall risk profile should be reduced compared to if the firm were to invest in enterprises at the low end of the range.

Management has made sure, over time, heavily diversify the company. Its top ten largest positions account for only 23% of its portfolio. What’s more, 73% of its portfolio is invested in first lien debt, while 5% is tied up in second lien debt. All told, about 79% of its portfolio is invested in senior secured securities. Outside of that, it does have some exposure to preferred equity, unsecured debt, common equity, and joint ventures. But when you add all of this together, we end up with a well-diversified prospect that, thanks to the business model of the company, allows it to capture a pretty hefty yield.

As interest rates have dropped, we have started to see a decline in the weighted average total yield that the company has been able to get from its investments. Back in 2023, for instance, this was 11.9% per annum. But by the end of 2025, that number had fallen to 9.5%. This might seem small. But when we are talking about a portfolio that consists of $16.47 billion worth of assets, it adds up in a big way. Management does help to boost returns by taking on a bit of leverage. But the net debt-to-equity ratio of the company at the end of 2025 was about 1.19. That's not exactly high. But it does help to explain the greater yield that the company is currently paying out.

One thing that has been helpful for shareholders is the fact that, over time, management has succeeded in growing overall net assets. The firm ended 2025 with $7.40 billion worth, with investments at fair value worth $16.47 billion. These numbers compare favorably to the $5.95 billion and $13.19 billion, respectively, that the firm had at the end of 2024. This has allowed a general increase in financial performance. In fact, in the chart below, you can see investment income, net investment income, unrealized gains, realized gains, and a change in net assets, for the company for the last four completed fiscal years now. 2025 saw a $627.4 million increase in net assets for the company thanks to robust net investment income and a very small uptick in unrealized gains. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that unrealized losses totaled $179.2 million.

To better understand what is going on here, I would like to point you to the chart below. In it, you can see dividends, changes in net assets, distributions, and other metrics, that I calculated for the last few years now. What we see here is that the company continues to pay out its change in net assets to shareholders in the form of distributions. That's not surprising. In fact, that's the entire point of this business model. It's also the only mechanism through which it's yield could be even remotely close to as high as it is now.

The problem with this approach, however, is that in order to grow, Blue Owl Capital must necessarily issue additional shares. In fact, from the final quarter of 2024 to the final quarter of 2025, the overall number of shares outstanding jumped 28%. In many instances that I have looked at in the past, this means a deterioration of shareholder value. But as the chart below illustrates, NAV per share has been pretty consistent. Admittedly, the $15.26 reported for the end of 2024 was higher than the $14.81 that the company had in 2025. And when you consider that, lately, the price per unit for the company has been a bit on the weak side of things, the end result has been a bit of deterioration in shareholder value. But this is not massive in the grand scheme of things, especially compared to other investments similar to this that I have analyzed in the past. In those cases, I often assign the companies or funds in question a ‘strong sell’ rating.

Some of the pressure that the company has likely faced lately has come from some drama regarding Blue Owl Capital Corporation II, which is a privately held BDC that is also managed by the parent business known as Blue Owl Capital. This is a fairly illiquid retail private credit fund. Earlier this year, the parent, Blue Owl Capital, shut the door on investor withdrawals from that fund and ended quarterly redemptions. This was rather significant news, with Mohamed El-Erian, who is one of the most well-known individuals in finance, warning that this could be a ‘canary in the coal mine’ moment for the private credit market. The private credit space is estimated to be worth around $3 trillion, making it a major segment of global finance. And by the year 2030, it's expected to expand further to $4 trillion in size. But since the final quarter of 2025, withdrawal requests for large BDCs have surged 200%. This comes as ‘shadow default rates’ for companies that use payment and kind debt have more than doubled since 2021. Some of this is likely because of broader economic weakness. However, declining interest rates are compressing overall returns, as I detailed already.

After this decision by management, Blue Owl Capital made the decision to sell $1.4 billion of its loans in order to raise liquidity. At first glance, this seemed great. This was especially because these assets were sold at 99.7% of par value. Some of the drama, however, comes from the fact that $400 million of this $1.4 billion sale came from Blue Owl Capital specifically. Plus, one of the buyers was none other than an affiliate of Blue Owl Capital. This likely stoked fears that the same family of companies is shifting capital around in order to reduce concerns about the overall liquidity of the businesses in question. This can be perceived as being very similar to moving around the chairs on the deck of the Titanic. To be clear, this does not mean that Blue Owl Capital itself is at financial risk. But the optics of it were not exactly great.

Another problem is that, historically, Blue Owl Capital has been a loser. In the chart above, you can see its overall return compared to the S&P 500 and even the equal weighted index for the S&P 500 over the last few years. And in the chart below, you can see what its return has been relative to these indices since I last wrote about it in December of last year. Considering that I view a ‘sell’ candidate as one that should underperform the market for the foreseeable future, the enterprise does seem to be matching that description quite nicely.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I would argue that Blue Owl Capital still does not make sense for investors to own. It continues to perform poorly, even with the hefty yield. We also have drama and broader economic conditions that should weigh on it moving forward. This combination of factors justifies a more cautious approach in my view. That's why I think that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Janus Henderson Opportunistic Alpha Managed Account Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned -3.67% (gross) and the S&P 500® Index returned 2.66%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology sector detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in the consumer staples sector contributed.

Outlook

Our differentiated investment approach aims to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broad economy.

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news. Economic growth has been relatively stable despite a government shutdown, policy headwinds, and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) followed a September rate cut with two additional 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of returns early in the quarter. The market began to broaden in the second half of October as concerns over valuations and potential investment returns led to declines in AI-related stocks. Nonetheless, stocks ended the year on a strong note.

Portfolio review

Oracle (ORCL) was relative detractor, amid volatility in AI-related stocks. Oracle has emerged as a leading player in the AI market, supported by its strong and growing position in the hyperscale market as well as close relationships with leading AI partners. Oracle's cloud business has signed several multibillion-dollar contracts. This led to a large increase in remaining performance obligations (RPOs), which represent expected future revenues. Despite the company's strong financial performance, the stock declined in the fourth quarter as investors grew more cautious about Oracle's ability to fund the ambitious infrastructure buildout necessary to service these future contracts. Further, the market has concerns about the potential customer concentration within these RPOs. We believe these concerns are overstated as Oracle has multiple avenues for funding growth due to its solid balance sheet and strong operating cash flow. In our view, the company remains well positioned to benefit from the ongoing buildout of AI capacity because of its technological advantages and strategic business relationships.

Surgery Partners (SGRY), another relative detractor, is the largest U.S. owner and operator of ambulatory surgical centers (ASCs). The company has benefited from a growing shift in surgery volumes away from hospitals and toward ASC facilities, which offer advantages for doctors, patients, and insurers compared to hospital-based procedures. While the company reported solid third-quarter earnings, shares declined after the management team warned of near-term headwinds for its commercial business. Investors also have worried about how the suspension of Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies may impact demand for medical procedures more broadly. We believe these concerns were overstated. Surgery Partners has minimal exposure to the ACA side of the market, and we have continued to see signs of stable growth in its core market. We remain invested in the stock.

Capital One Financial (COF) was a top positive contributor. The financial services company has delivered strong performance, aided by robust credit demand and improved credit metrics. Its recent acquisition of Discover Financial Services has enhanced its competitive positioning while diversifying its business platform and expanding its technological capabilities. This merger also has allowed Capital One to move a significant amount of its debit card volumes onto the Discover network, which provides synergies and margin benefits. The company also has been returning money to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Madrigal Pharmaceuticals was another relative contributor. Madrigal has become a leader in the treatment of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), which is rapidly becoming one of the leading causes of liver failure in the U.S. The company has seen strong sales of Rezdiffra, the first FDA-approved medication for MASH that targets the accumulation of liver fat and inflammation. Sentiment around the drug has been positive, driven by strong physician adoption and payer reception trends.

Manager outlook

We are optimistic about the market environment in 2026, given the backdrop of continued stable economic growth. Consumer spending has remained resilient, especially for upper-income households benefiting from asset appreciation. While job growth has slowed, the unemployment rate remains below historic averages. Slower employment growth may also give the Fed more leeway to ease monetary policy, and lower interest rates could provide an economic tailwind for a broad range of companies. We see other potential drivers for market gains, including deregulation, tax policy changes, and a more accommodative environment for mergers and acquisitions.

We have continued to look for opportunity with long-term themes such as AI, which we see persisting regardless of macroeconomic conditions. Hyperscalers are still expanding capacity, and we believe the next phase of growth could shift toward software and data-rich companies that can monetize AI through productivity gains and new services.

While we continue to monitor such themes along with macroeconomic developments, our primary focus remains bottom-up stock selection. Our differentiated investment process aims to provide broad exposure across segments, sizes, and styles, avoiding reliance on any single theme. The core of our process seeks to unlock value through differentiated insights, especially around misunderstood business models, undervalued assets, and unappreciated growth potential. We remain on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of market volatility, as our long-term investment horizon and independent process allow us to capitalize on opportunities other investors might miss. Through this approach, we hope to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broad economy.
